content:
First, rephrase the condition in graph‐theoretic language.  Think of the $1000\times1000$ grid as the vertices of the usual grid‐graph $G$, with edges between horizontally or vertically adjacent squares.  A domino is then an edge of $G$, and the requirement that no two dominoes are adjacent (though they may touch at a corner) is exactly the statement that we want an induced matching in $G$—that is, a set of edges no two of which share a vertex or even have their endpoints joined by another edge of $G$.

It is known (and one can exhibit a simple $4\times4$–periodic pattern) that the maximum density of an induced matching in the infinite grid is $\tfrac14$.  Restricting to a finite $1000\times1000$ board (which is divisible by $4$ in each direction) one can tile it by $4\times4$ squares and in each $4\times4$ block select exactly one domino in “row‐2, columns $1\mod4$’’ and one in “column‐4, rows $3\mod4$’’ (for instance).  This places exactly $1$ domino per $4\times4$ block, i.e.\ $1$ domino per $16$ cells, hence density $1/4$.  Since there are $10^6$ cells, this produces
\[
\frac{10^6}{4}\;=\;250\,000
\]
dominoes.  One also shows by a simple counting/averaging argument that no induced matching can exceed density $1/4$ on the grid, so this construction is best possible.

Answer: \boxed{250000}\,.
